question:
4. For each positive integer \( n \), put
\[
p_{n}=(1+1 / n)^{n}, P_{n}=(1+1 / n)^{n+1}, h_{n}=\frac{2 p_{n} P_{n}}{p_{n}+P_{n}}
\]

Prove that \( h_{1}<h_{2}<\cdots<h_{n}<\cdots \).
solution:
First Solution. We find \( h_{n}=2(n+1)^{n+1} n^{-n}(2 n+1)^{-1} \). Consider the function \( g \) defined by
\[
g(x)=\log 2+(x+1) \log (x+1)-x \log x-\log (2 x+1)
\]

We have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
g^{\prime}(x)=\log (x+1)-\log x-\frac{2}{2 x+1} \\
g^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x+1}-\frac{1}{x}+\frac{4}{(2 x+1)^{2}}=-\frac{1}{x(x+1)(2 x+1)^{2}}<0
\end{array}
\]
for \( 0<x<\infty \). Hence \( g^{\prime} \) decreases on \( (0, \infty) \). Since
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} g^{\prime}(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \log \left(\frac{x+1}{x}\right)-\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{2 x+1}=0
\]
it follows that \( g^{\prime} \) is positive on \( (0, \infty) \). Therefore \( g \) increases on \( (0, \infty) \), so \( h_{n}=\exp g(n) \) is strictly increasing for positive integers \( n \).

Second Solution. We find
\[
\frac{h_{n}}{h_{n-1}}=\left(1-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right)^{n} \cdot \frac{1+\frac{1}{n}}{1-\frac{1}{n}} \cdot \frac{1-\frac{1}{2 n}}{1+\frac{1}{2 n}}
\]

Since
\[
\log (1-x)=-\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{x^{k}}{k}
\]
and
\[
\log \frac{1+x}{1-x}=2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2 k-1}}{2 k-1}
\]
for \( |x|<1 \), it follows that for \( n>1 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\log \frac{h_{n}}{h_{n-1}}= & -n \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k n^{2 k}}+2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 k-1) n^{2 k-1}} \\
& -2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 k-1)(2 n)^{2 k-1}} \\
= & \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{2 k-1}}\left(-\frac{1}{k}+\frac{2}{2 k-1}-\frac{1}{(2 k-1) 2^{2 k-2}}\right) \\
= & \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{2 k-1}} \frac{2^{2 k-2}-k}{k(2 k-1) 2^{2 k-2}}>0
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( h_{n}>h_{n-1} \) for \( n>1 \), so \( h_{1}<h_{2}<h_{3}<\cdots \).
Remark. Since \( h_{n} \) is the harmonic mean between \( p_{n} \) and \( P_{n} \), and \( \lim p_{n} \) \( =\lim P_{n}=e \), it follows that \( \lim h_{n}=e \).

descriptive_long.question:
4. For each positive integer \( indexvar \), put
\[
powseqterm=(1+1 / indexvar)^{indexvar},\; powseqnext=(1+1 / indexvar)^{indexvar+1},\; harmseqterm=\frac{2\,powseqterm\,powseqnext}{powseqterm+powseqnext}
\]

Prove that \( harmsecone<harmsectwo<\cdots<harmseqterm<\cdots \).
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. We find \( harmseqterm=2(indexvar+1)^{indexvar+1}\,indexvar^{-indexvar}(2 indexvar+1)^{-1} \). Consider the function \( logfuncg \) defined by
\[
logfuncg(realvarx)=\log 2+(realvarx+1)\log(realvarx+1)-realvarx\,\log realvarx-\log(2 realvarx+1)
\]

We have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
logfuncg^{\prime}(realvarx)=\log(realvarx+1)-\log realvarx-\frac{2}{2 realvarx+1} \\
logfuncg^{\prime\prime}(realvarx)=\frac{1}{realvarx+1}-\frac{1}{realvarx}+\frac{4}{(2 realvarx+1)^{2}}=-\frac{1}{realvarx(realvarx+1)(2 realvarx+1)^{2}}<0
\end{array}
\]
for \( 0<realvarx<\infty \). Hence \( logfuncg^{\prime} \) decreases on \( (0,\infty) \). Since
\[
\lim_{realvarx\to\infty}logfuncg^{\prime}(realvarx)=\lim_{realvarx\to\infty}\log\!\left(\frac{realvarx+1}{realvarx}\right)-\lim_{realvarx\to\infty}\frac{2}{2 realvarx+1}=0,
\]
it follows that \( logfuncg^{\prime} \) is positive on \( (0,\infty) \). Therefore \( logfuncg \) increases on \( (0,\infty) \), so \( harmseqterm=\exp logfuncg(indexvar) \) is strictly increasing for positive integers \( indexvar \).

Second Solution. We find
\[
\frac{harmseqterm}{harmseqprev}=\left(1-\frac{1}{indexvar^{2}}\right)^{indexvar}\cdot\frac{1+\frac{1}{indexvar}}{1-\frac{1}{indexvar}}\cdot\frac{1-\frac{1}{2 indexvar}}{1+\frac{1}{2 indexvar}}.
\]

Since
\[
\log(1-realvarx)=-\sum_{sumindexk=1}^{\infty}\frac{realvarx^{sumindexk}}{sumindexk},
\qquad
\log\frac{1+realvarx}{1-realvarx}=2\sum_{sumindexk=1}^{\infty}\frac{realvarx^{2 sumindexk-1}}{2 sumindexk-1},
\]
for \( |realvarx|<1 \), it follows that for \( indexvar>1 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\log\frac{harmseqterm}{harmseqprev}=\;&-indexvar\sum_{sumindexk=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{sumindexk\,indexvar^{2 sumindexk}}+2\sum_{sumindexk=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2 sumindexk-1)\,indexvar^{2 sumindexk-1}}\\
&-2\sum_{sumindexk=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2 sumindexk-1)(2 indexvar)^{2 sumindexk-1}}\\
=\;&\sum_{sumindexk=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{indexvar^{2 sumindexk-1}}\left(-\frac{1}{sumindexk}+\frac{2}{2 sumindexk-1}-\frac{1}{(2 sumindexk-1)2^{2 sumindexk-2}}\right)\\
=\;&\sum_{sumindexk=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{indexvar^{2 sumindexk-1}}\,\frac{2^{2 sumindexk-2}-sumindexk}{sumindexk(2 sumindexk-1)2^{2 sumindexk-2}}>0.
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( harmseqterm>harmseqprev \) for \( indexvar>1 \), so \( harmsecone<harmsectwo<harmsecthre<\cdots \).

Remark. Since \( harmseqterm \) is the harmonic mean between \( powseqterm \) and \( powseqnext \), and \( \lim powseqterm=\lim powseqnext=e \), it follows that \( \lim harmseqterm=e \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
4. For each positive integer \( wanderers \), put
\[
    sapphire=(1+1 / wanderers)^{wanderers}, \; buttercup=(1+1 / wanderers)^{wanderers+1}, \; sandstone=\frac{2\,sapphire\,buttercup}{sapphire+buttercup}
\]

Prove that \( cloverleaf<starlighter<\cdots<sandstone<\cdots \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. We find \( sandstone=2(wanderers+1)^{wanderers+1} wanderers^{-wanderers}(2 wanderers+1)^{-1} \). Consider the function \( lumberjack \) defined by
\[
    lumberjack(dragonfly)=\log 2+(dragonfly+1) \log (dragonfly+1)-dragonfly \log dragonfly-\log (2 dragonfly+1)
\]

We have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
    lumberjack^{\prime}(dragonfly)=\log (dragonfly+1)-\log dragonfly-\frac{2}{2 dragonfly+1} \\
    lumberjack^{\prime\prime}(dragonfly)=\frac{1}{dragonfly+1}-\frac{1}{dragonfly}+\frac{4}{(2 dragonfly+1)^{2}}=-\frac{1}{dragonfly(dragonfly+1)(2 dragonfly+1)^{2}}<0
\end{array}
\]
for \( 0<dragonfly<\infty \). Hence \( lumberjack^{\prime} \) decreases on \( (0, \infty) \). Since
\[
    \lim_{dragonfly\to\infty} lumberjack^{\prime}(dragonfly)=\lim_{dragonfly\to\infty} \log\!\left(\frac{dragonfly+1}{dragonfly}\right)-\lim_{dragonfly\to\infty} \frac{2}{2 dragonfly+1}=0
\]
it follows that \( lumberjack^{\prime} \) is positive on \( (0, \infty) \). Therefore \( lumberjack \) increases on \( (0, \infty) \), so \( sandstone=\exp lumberjack(wanderers) \) is strictly increasing for positive integers \( wanderers \).

Second Solution. We find
\[
    \frac{sandstone}{ironsmith}=\left(1-\frac{1}{wanderers^{2}}\right)^{wanderers} \cdot \frac{1+\frac{1}{wanderers}}{1-\frac{1}{wanderers}} \cdot \frac{1-\frac{1}{2 wanderers}}{1+\frac{1}{2 wanderers}}
\]

Since
\[
    \log(1-dragonfly)=-\sum_{raincloud=1}^{\infty} \frac{dragonfly^{raincloud}}{raincloud}
\]
and
\[
    \log\frac{1+dragonfly}{1-dragonfly}=2 \sum_{raincloud=1}^{\infty} \frac{dragonfly^{2 raincloud-1}}{2 raincloud-1}
\]
for \( |dragonfly|<1 \), it follows that for \( wanderers>1 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
    \log\frac{sandstone}{ironsmith}= & -wanderers \sum_{raincloud=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{raincloud\,wanderers^{2 raincloud}}+2 \sum_{raincloud=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 raincloud-1) wanderers^{2 raincloud-1}} \\
    & -2 \sum_{raincloud=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 raincloud-1)(2 wanderers)^{2 raincloud-1}} \\
    = & \sum_{raincloud=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{wanderers^{2 raincloud-1}}\left(-\frac{1}{raincloud}+\frac{2}{2 raincloud-1}-\frac{1}{(2 raincloud-1) 2^{2 raincloud-2}}\right) \\
    = & \sum_{raincloud=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{wanderers^{2 raincloud-1}} \frac{2^{2 raincloud-2}-raincloud}{raincloud(2 raincloud-1) 2^{2 raincloud-2}}>0
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( sandstone>ironsmith \) for \( wanderers>1 \), so \( cloverleaf<starlighter<mirrorball<\cdots \).

Remark. Since sandstone is the harmonic mean between sapphire and buttercup, and \( \lim sapphire=\lim buttercup=e \), it follows that \( \lim sandstone=e \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
4. For each positive integer \( endlesscount \), put
\[
vanishseq_{endlesscount}=(1+1 / endlesscount)^{endlesscount},\; shrinkseq_{endlesscount}=(1+1 / endlesscount)^{endlesscount+1},\; discordseq_{endlesscount}=\frac{2\, vanishseq_{endlesscount}\, shrinkseq_{endlesscount}}{vanishseq_{endlesscount}+shrinkseq_{endlesscount}}
\]

Prove that \( solediscord<duodiscord<\cdots<discordseq_{endlesscount}<\cdots \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. We find \( discordseq_{endlesscount}=2(endlesscount+1)^{endlesscount+1} endlesscount^{-endlesscount}(2\,endlesscount+1)^{-1} \). Consider the function \( decayfunc \) defined by
\[
decayfunc(staticvar)=\log 2+(staticvar+1)\log(staticvar+1)-staticvar\log staticvar-\log(2\,staticvar+1)
\]

We have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
decayfunc^{\prime}(staticvar)=\log(staticvar+1)-\log staticvar-\frac{2}{2\,staticvar+1} \\
decayfunc^{\prime\prime}(staticvar)=\frac{1}{staticvar+1}-\frac{1}{staticvar}+\frac{4}{(2\,staticvar+1)^{2}}=-\frac{1}{staticvar(staticvar+1)(2\,staticvar+1)^{2}}<0
\end{array}
\]
for \( 0<staticvar<\infty \). Hence \( decayfunc^{\prime} \) decreases on \( (0,\infty) \). Since
\[
\lim_{staticvar\to\infty}decayfunc^{\prime}(staticvar)=\lim_{staticvar\to\infty}\log\left(\frac{staticvar+1}{staticvar}\right)-\lim_{staticvar\to\infty}\frac{2}{2\,staticvar+1}=0
\]
it follows that \( decayfunc^{\prime} \) is positive on \( (0,\infty) \). Therefore \( decayfunc \) increases on \( (0,\infty) \), so \( discordseq_{endlesscount}=\exp decayfunc(endlesscount) \) is strictly increasing for positive integers \( endlesscount \).

Second Solution. We find
\[
\frac{discordseq_{endlesscount}}{prediscord}=\left(1-\frac{1}{endlesscount^{2}}\right)^{endlesscount}\cdot\frac{1+\frac{1}{endlesscount}}{1-\frac{1}{endlesscount}}\cdot\frac{1-\frac{1}{2\,endlesscount}}{1+\frac{1}{2\,endlesscount}}
\]

Since
\[
\log(1-staticvar)=-\sum_{randomizer=1}^{\infty}\frac{staticvar^{randomizer}}{randomizer}
\]
and
\[
\log\frac{1+staticvar}{1-staticvar}=2\sum_{randomizer=1}^{\infty}\frac{staticvar^{2\,randomizer-1}}{2\,randomizer-1}
\]
for \( |staticvar|<1 \), it follows that for \( endlesscount>1 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\log\frac{discordseq_{endlesscount}}{prediscord}= & -endlesscount\sum_{randomizer=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{randomizer\,endlesscount^{2\,randomizer}}+2\sum_{randomizer=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2\,randomizer-1)endlesscount^{2\,randomizer-1}}\\
& -2\sum_{randomizer=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2\,randomizer-1)(2\,endlesscount)^{2\,randomizer-1}} \\
= & \sum_{randomizer=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{endlesscount^{2\,randomizer-1}}\left(-\frac{1}{randomizer}+\frac{2}{2\,randomizer-1}-\frac{1}{(2\,randomizer-1)2^{2\,randomizer-2}}\right) \\
= & \sum_{randomizer=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{endlesscount^{2\,randomizer-1}}\frac{2^{2\,randomizer-2}-randomizer}{randomizer(2\,randomizer-1)2^{2\,randomizer-2}}>0
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( discordseq_{endlesscount}>prediscord \) for \( endlesscount>1 \), so \( solediscord<duodiscord<tridiscord<\cdots \).

Remark. Since \( discordseq_{endlesscount} \) is the harmonic mean between \( vanishseq_{endlesscount} \) and \( shrinkseq_{endlesscount} \), and \( \lim vanishseq_{endlesscount}=\lim shrinkseq_{endlesscount}=e \), it follows that \( \lim discordseq_{endlesscount}=e \).

garbled_string.question:
For each positive integer \( qzxwvtnp \), put
\[
hjgrksla=(1+1 / qzxwvtnp)^{qzxwvtnp},\ lmdkqweo=(1+1 / qzxwvtnp)^{qzxwvtnp+1},\ suifvgab=\frac{2 hjgrksla lmdkqweo}{hjgrksla+lmdkqweo}
\]

Prove that \( uapcnehr<rvokzyut<\cdots<suifvgab<\cdots \).
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. We find \( suifvgab=2(qzxwvtnp+1)^{qzxwvtnp+1} qzxwvtnp^{-qzxwvtnp}(2 qzxwvtnp+1)^{-1} \). Consider the function \( ojnefkli \) defined by
\[
ojnefkli(mwbtrhac)=\log 2+(mwbtrhac+1) \log (mwbtrhac+1)-mwbtrhac \log mwbtrhac-\log (2 mwbtrhac+1)
\]

We have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
ojnefkli^{\prime}(mwbtrhac)=\log (mwbtrhac+1)-\log mwbtrhac-\frac{2}{2 mwbtrhac+1} \\
ojnefkli^{\prime \prime}(mwbtrhac)=\frac{1}{mwbtrhac+1}-\frac{1}{mwbtrhac}+\frac{4}{(2 mwbtrhac+1)^{2}}=-\frac{1}{mwbtrhac(mwbtrhac+1)(2 mwbtrhac+1)^{2}}<0
\end{array}
\]
for \( 0<mwbtrhac<\infty \). Hence \( ojnefkli^{\prime} \) decreases on \( (0, \infty) \). Since
\[
\lim _{mwbtrhac \rightarrow \infty} ojnefkli^{\prime}(mwbtrhac)=\lim _{mwbtrhac \rightarrow \infty} \log \left(\frac{mwbtrhac+1}{mwbtrhac}\right)-\lim _{mwbtrhac \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{2 mwbtrhac+1}=0
\]
it follows that \( ojnefkli^{\prime} \) is positive on \( (0, \infty) \). Therefore \( ojnefkli \) increases on \( (0, \infty) \), so \( suifvgab=\exp ojnefkli(qzxwvtnp) \) is strictly increasing for positive integers \( qzxwvtnp \).

Second Solution. We find
\[
\frac{suifvgab}{gdvczmra}=\left(1-\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp^{2}}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} \cdot \frac{1+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}}{1-\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}} \cdot \frac{1-\frac{1}{2 qzxwvtnp}}{1+\frac{1}{2 qzxwvtnp}}
\]

Since
\[
\log (1-mwbtrhac)=-\sum_{zeiygnus=1}^{\infty} \frac{mwbtrhac^{zeiygnus}}{zeiygnus}
\]
and
\[
\log \frac{1+mwbtrhac}{1-mwbtrhac}=2 \sum_{zeiygnus=1}^{\infty} \frac{mwbtrhac^{2 zeiygnus-1}}{2 zeiygnus-1}
\]
for \( |mwbtrhac|<1 \), it follows that for \( qzxwvtnp>1 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\log \frac{suifvgab}{gdvczmra}= & -qzxwvtnp \sum_{zeiygnus=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{zeiygnus qzxwvtnp^{2 zeiygnus}}+2 \sum_{zeiygnus=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 zeiygnus-1) qzxwvtnp^{2 zeiygnus-1}} \\
& -2 \sum_{zeiygnus=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 zeiygnus-1)(2 qzxwvtnp)^{2 zeiygnus-1}} \\
= & \sum_{zeiygnus=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp^{2 zeiygnus-1}}\left(-\frac{1}{zeiygnus}+\frac{2}{2 zeiygnus-1}-\frac{1}{(2 zeiygnus-1) 2^{2 zeiygnus-2}}\right) \\
= & \sum_{zeiygnus=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp^{2 zeiygnus-1}} \frac{2^{2 zeiygnus-2}-zeiygnus}{zeiygnus(2 zeiygnus-1) 2^{2 zeiygnus-2}}>0
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( suifvgab>gdvczmra \) for \( qzxwvtnp>1 \), so \( uapcnehr<rvokzyut<ptlaqnwi<\cdots \).

Remark. Since \( suifvgab \) is the harmonic mean between \( hjgrksla \) and \( lmdkqweo \), and \( \lim hjgrksla =\lim lmdkqweo=e \), it follows that \( \lim suifvgab=e \).

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer m \geq  2.  For every positive integer n set  
 a_n = (1+1/n)^n, A_n = (1+1/n)^{n+1}, and define the weighted m-harmonic mean  
  s_n^{(m)} =  (m+2) a_n^{\,m}A_n /(a_n^{\,m}+A_n^{\,m}).  

(a) Show that, for each fixed m, the sequence (s_n^{(m)})_{n\geq 1} is strictly increasing.  
(b) Prove  lim_{n\to \infty } s_n^{(m)} = e.  
(c) If m_1>m_2 \geq  2, prove s_n^{(m_1)} > s_n^{(m_2)} for every n.
kernel_variant.solution:
(\approx  185 words)

Step 1.  Closed form.  
a_n^{\,m}A_n=(n+1)^{(2n+1)m}/n^{(2n+1)m} and  
a_n^{\,m}+A_n^{\,m}=(n+1)^{nm}/n^{nm}\cdot (m n+1)/n.  
Hence  
 s_n^{(m)}=(m+2)(n+1)^{(n+1)m}/[n^{nm}(m n+1)].             (\star )

Step 2.  A continuous companion.  
For x>0 put  
 S_{m}(x)=(m+2)(x+1)^{(x+1)m}\cdot x^{-xm}(m x+1)^{-1},  
 g_{m}(x)=ln S_{m}(x).

Step 3.  Derivatives.  
g'_{m}(x)=m[ln(x+1)-ln x]-m/(m x+1);  
g''_{m}(x)=m[1/(x+1)-1/x]+m^2/(m x+1)^2  
     =-m/(x(x+1)(m x+1)^2)<0 (x>0).  
So g'_{m} is strictly decreasing.

Step 4.  Sign of g'_{m}.  
Because ln(x+1)-ln x ~ 1/x while m/(m x+1)~1/x, we obtain  
 lim_{x\to \infty }g'_{m}(x)=0.  
With g'_{m} decreasing towards 0 we have g'_{m}(x)>0 for every x>0.  
Therefore g_{m} is strictly increasing, and by (\star )  
 s_1^{(m)}<s_2^{(m)}<\cdots  - proving (a).

Step 5.  Limit.  
Since a_n\to e and A_n\to e, both numerator and denominator in s_n^{(m)} tend to e^{m+1}; their ratio tends to 1, hence lim s_n^{(m)}=e, establishing (b).

Step 6.  Dependence on m.  
For fixed x the map m\mapsto g_{m}(x) is increasing because \partial g_{m}/\partial m = ln(x+1)-ln x-1/(m x+1)>0.  
Thus, for m_1>m_2 and every integer n, g_{m_1}(n)>g_{m_2}(n) \Rightarrow  s_n^{(m_1)}>s_n^{(m_2)}, yielding (c). \blacksquare

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
